pattern: Each candidate base corresponds to writing n as a string of m ones, so iterate the digit count from large to small and solve for the base that makes the repunit equal n.
hints: Writing n as all 1's in base k for m digits means n = 1 + k + k^2 + ... + k^(m-1); the more digits you use, the smaller the base, so the smallest base comes from the largest digit count.
For a fixed number of digits m, the base k is pinned down: k is roughly the (m-1)-th root of n, so you only have one candidate to test per m.
Loop m from about 60 down to 2; estimate k = floor(n^(1/(m-1))), verify 1 + k + ... + k^(m-1) == n, and return the first (largest-m) match; the fallback m = 2 always works with base n - 1.

edge_inputs.description: 4681 is five ones in base eight
huge value whose only good base is n minus one
smallest allowed n, base is n minus one
power-of-two-minus-one style repunit in base two
value with many digits favours a small base

stress.description: construct n as a guaranteed repunit so a non-trivial base exists
stress.note: n built as a sum of powers so at least one good base is non-trivial
